book_claims.claim: Zinc is important for normal immune function; deficiency impairs immune response.
book_claims.mechanism: Zinc is an essential cofactor for over 300 enzymes, including those involved in immune cell development (T-cell differentiation, natural killer cell activity), wound healing, and DNA repair. Deficiency leads to thymic atrophy and impaired adaptive immunity.

book_claims.claim: Zinc is listed alongside vitamin D as an important micronutrient for normal immune function, and alongside iron for immune support.
book_claims.mechanism: Zinc and vitamin D synergistically support innate and adaptive immunity; both are required for cytokine regulation and pathogen defense. Zinc and iron together are critical for hematopoiesis and oxygen transport.

book_claims.claim: Longo recommends obtaining zinc through legumes, nuts, and seafood, supplemented via a multivitamin every 2–3 days.
book_claims.mechanism: Zinc from animal sources (oysters, red meat) has superior bioavailability vs plant sources due to phytate chelation; diversity of sources and occasional supplementation ensures sufficiency on a plant-forward diet.

supplementary_research.finding: PMID 30949907: Meta-analysis of 28 trials found zinc supplementation significantly reduces the duration and severity of the common cold; supports the book's emphasis on zinc for immune resilience.

PMID 25369925: Zinc supplementation in elderly subjects restored thymic function, improved T-cell counts, and reduced oxidative stress markers, demonstrating a direct anti-immunosenescence effect relevant to longevity.

PMID 28181472: Zinc acts as an epigenetic regulator and DNA damage response activator; inadequate zinc status is associated with increased genomic instability, a hallmark of aging — extending the longevity relevance beyond immunology.

Examine confirms that zinc picolinate and zinc gluconate have superior bioavailability to zinc oxide; optimal supplemental dose is 25–45 mg/day; chronic high doses (>100 mg/day) can impair copper absorption.

culinary_pairings.source: Highest food source: ~39–74 mg per 3 oz cooked
Plant source: ~2.9 mg per 28 g; top plant-based zinc food
Lentils ~2.5 mg per 100 g cooked; chickpeas ~1.5 mg per 100 g cooked

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: From food: bioavailability from animal sources is ~40–50%; from plant sources ~15–26% due to phytate inhibition. Zinc picolinate and gluconate are most bioavailable supplemental forms.
Zinc is a structural and catalytic component of >300 enzymes including superoxide dismutase (antioxidant), RNA polymerase (gene expression), and matrix metalloproteinases (tissue remodeling).

synergies.description: Both zinc and vitamin D are required for normal immune function; zinc supports T-cell activation while vitamin D modulates inflammatory cytokine production. Deficiency of either impairs immunity; co-supplementation is synergistic.

Zinc and iron are co-listed in the Longevity Diet as critical immune micronutrients; however, high-dose zinc supplementation (>50 mg/day) can competitively inhibit iron absorption — balance is important.
Pumpkin seeds are the most practical whole-food zinc source for plant-based diets; adding them to salads or grain bowls provides both zinc and magnesium.
